title: PHP object injection via unserialize() on imported slider backup data
auth_required: administrator (smartslider_edit + unfiltered_html) - or social engineering admin into importing a malicious .ss3 file
entry_point: ImportSlider::import() called from ControllerAjaxSliders::actionImport() and ControllerAjaxSlider::actionImportDemo()
root_cause: Line 81: $this->backup = unserialize($importData['data']); is called on data extracted from an uploaded .ss3 zip file after only a custom Serialize\Parser::isValidData() check. PHP's unserialize() will instantiate arbitrary objects from any class loaded in the current request context. If a suitable POP (Property-Oriented Programming) gadget chain exists in the WordPress core, any loaded plugin, or this plugin's own classes (742 PHP files), an attacker can craft a malicious .ss3 file to trigger arbitrary code execution during __destruct, __wakeup, or __toString magic method calls.

attack_vector_1.name: Direct admin exploit
steps: 1. Identify a POP chain in loaded PHP classes (WordPress core, other plugins, or Smart Slider 3 itself)
2. Craft a serialized PHP payload that triggers the POP chain
3. Package it as a valid .ss3 zip file (zip with 'data' entry containing the serialized payload)
4. Import via the Smart Slider admin interface (Sliders > Import)
5. unserialize() instantiates attacker-controlled objects, triggering the POP chain

attack_vector_2.name: Social engineering / supply chain
steps: 3. Admin imports the .ss3 file via the import interface
4. Code executes with web server privileges

exploitation.notes: The Serialize\Parser isValidData() check may block some payloads but is unlikely to prevent all POP chains. Common WP POP chains are documented in phpggc (PHP Generic Gadget Chains) for WordPress/Yoast/etc. The actionImportDemo() method also calls ImportSlider::import() with data fetched from smartslider3.com, but that endpoint verifies the URL, so SSRF-to-deserialization is not directly possible.

impact: Medium/High depending on available POP chains. If exploitable POP chain exists: Remote Code Execution as web server user (unauthenticated RCE if social engineering succeeds, authenticated RCE for admins). Without a POP chain: impact is limited to unexpected object instantiation effects.

remediation.primary: Replace unserialize() with json_decode() by migrating the .ss3 format to JSON. This eliminates the entire class of object injection.
